coredata.dc:title: A novel electrochemiluminescence sensor for the detection of nitroaniline based on the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Shufan
Chen, Xueqian
Xia, Tingting
Ma, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), as a new class of carbon nanomaterials, have potential application in sensor, fuel cells, optoelectronics field due to their stable photoluminescence (PL) and electrocatalytic activity. Herein, a facile novel electrochemiluminescence (ECL) signal-on method for nitroaniline (NA) sensing based on N-GQDs and chitosan was developed. Chitosan displays high water permeability, hydrophilic property and good adhesion to load the N-GQDs to the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface. N-GQDs have shown as highly active reagent and catalyst for rapid diazotization reaction of anilines. When NA was added to the electrolyte solution consisting of mineral acid and sodium nitrite, N-GQDs/chitosan modified electrode exhibited obvious enhancement of ECL intensity, which was ascribed to the occurrence of diazotization reaction of NA. Therefore, NA can be detected with high selectivity based on the N-GQDs/chitosan ECL system. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first report about the NA detection based on the catalysis and ECL capabilities of N-GQDs. There was a wide linear ECL intensity response ranging from 0.01 to 1μmolL−1 NA. The practicability of the ECL sensing platform in real water samples has shown the satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Electrochemiluminescence
Nitroaniline
Diazotization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The characterization of the prepared N-GQDs
The characterization and electrochemiluminescence behavior of chitosan/N-GQDs system
The optimization of ECL detection conditions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the as-prepared N-GQDs. (B) Fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs. Inset: photograph image of N-GQDs solution under UV lamp (365nm).

(A) SEM image of the chitosan/N-GQDs/GCE electrode. (B) EIS of (a) bare GCE, (b) N-GQDs/GCE, (c) chitosan/N-GQDs/GCE in 0.1molL−1 KCl solution containing 2.5mmolL−1 K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]. Inset: the magnification image of curve a. The impedance spectra were recorded in the frequency range of 0.01Hz–100kHz with the amplitude of 5mV. (C) CVs of bare GCE (a) and chitosan-N-GQDs modified electrode (b) in 0.1molL−1 KCl solution containing 1.0mmolL−1 K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] at 0.1V/S. (D) The ECL intensity of chitosan/N-GQDs/GCE in PBS solution (curve a), PBS solution with the presence of 1μmolL−1 of DA (curve b), 10mmolL−1 of HCl solution (curve c), mixture of HCl (10mmolL−1) and NaNO2 (10μmolL−1) (curve d), the mixed solution of HCl and NaNO2 with the presence of 1 μmol L−1 of DA (curve e).

Selectivity of this ECL sensor for NA over other organic compounds: concentrations of them were 0.5μmolL−1. I0 and I were the ECL intensity of chitosan/N-GQDs/GCE sensor without and with addition of those organic compounds.
(A) ECL time trace of chitosan/N-GQDs/GCE to different concentrations of NA (from left to right: 0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.05, 0.08, 0.1, 0.5, 1μmolL−1). (B) Calibration curve for quantification of NA.
Schematic presentation of electrode modification and evaluation procedure.
Determination of NA in water samples (n=3).

head.ce:title: A novel electrochemiluminescence sensor for the detection of nitroaniline based on the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), as a new class of carbon nanomaterials, have potential application in sensor, fuel cells, optoelectronics field due to their stable photoluminescence (PL) and electrocatalytic activity. Herein, a facile novel electrochemiluminescence (ECL) signal-on method for nitroaniline (NA) sensing based on N-GQDs and chitosan was developed. Chitosan displays high water permeability, hydrophilic property and good adhesion to load the N-GQDs to the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface. N-GQDs have shown as highly active reagent and catalyst for rapid diazotization reaction of anilines. When NA was added to the electrolyte solution consisting of mineral acid and sodium nitrite, N-GQDs/chitosan modified electrode exhibited obvious enhancement of ECL intensity, which was ascribed to the occurrence of diazotization reaction of NA. Therefore, NA can be detected with high selectivity based on the N-GQDs/chitosan ECL system. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first report about the NA detection based on the catalysis and ECL capabilities of N-GQDs. There was a wide linear ECL intensity response ranging from 0.01 to 1μmolL−1 NA. The practicability of the ECL sensing platform in real water samples has shown the satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitroaromatic compounds are environmental contaminants associated with anthropogenic activities (Chen et al., 2012). One important compound of this class is nitroaniline (NA). NA is an extremely important raw chemical that holds a non-substitutable place in the synthesis of a great variety of chemical products, including various organic dyes, pesticide, medicines, antiaging agents, photostabilizers, antioxidants and developing agents (Yuan et al., 2015). However, NA is strongly toxic comparable to that of trinitrotoluene (TNT) and trinitrophenol (TNP) and can be released into the environment directly as industry waste or indirectly as breakdown products of herbicides and pesticides (Guo et al., 2006). Due to the regarding strong electron-withdrawing groups, NA is poorly biodegradable in the environment (Chen et al., 2012). Furthermore, NA exists in natural world with readily permeating through soil and contaminate ground water. Research showed that NA can cause damage to DNA and react positive in mutagenicity tests (Huang et al., 2009). So, the highly sensitive and selective sensors for rapid and effective detection of NA is an extremely urgent issue concerning homeland security, environmental protection and humanitarian concerns. At present, various methods have been employed for the determination of NA, including capillary zone electrophoresis (Chen et al., 2012) and luminescent analytical methods (Yu et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015a, 2015b). However, these methods reported previously suffered from limitations of complicated synthetic process, time-consuming and high operational cost. Thus, more effective and facile analytical method for rapid and accurate determination of NA is urgently needed.

Recently, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) has received much attention and become an important detection method owing to its simplified set-up, low background, and high sensitivity (Ye et al., 2016). And fluorescence nanoparticles (e.g. QDs) with remarkable optoelectronic properties and excellent optical performance have been used in ECL sensing systems (Wang et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2015). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new type of luminescence nanoparticles, have sparked significant excitement owing to their superiority in terms of chemical inertness, ease of production, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility (Lin et al., 2014). These properties of GQDs have triggered their use in bioimaging, optical sensing and drug delivery (Ruiz et al., 2015; Zheng et al., 2015). However, bare GQDs without passivation of surface functionalization are usually possessed low observed quantum yield (QY) (Yuan et al., 2016). Doping GQDs with heteroatoms can change their intrinsic properties, including electronic characteristics, surface and local chemical features (Li et al., 2012; Chen et al., 2015). For example, the N atom, having a comparable atomic size and five valence electrons for bonding with carbon atoms, has been widely used for doping of GQDs. After nitrogen-doping, the PL quantum yields of N-GQDs can be improved largely (Tam et al., 2014; Li et al.,2015; Ju et al., 2014). N-GQDs with more active sites are ideal luminescence labels and promising catalytic roles. Recently, luminescent methods have been combined with electrochemical techniques to expand GQDs-based sensing applications (Liu et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2015). And researchers have established kinds of N-GQDs-based ECL sensors which were successfully used to detect small molecules, organic compounds, protein, and so on (Zhang et al., 2015a, 2015b; Zhou et al., 2015; Du et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015a, 2015b).

Chitosan is a well-known biocompatible and biodegradable nature polysaccharide with low toxicity and can easily form films (Chen et al., 2016). And chitosan displays high water permeability and hydrophilic property, strong hydrogel ability and good adhesion with the functional nanoparticles to the electrode surfaces (Liu et al., 2015). Other ECL sensors have been developed successfully based on chitosan thin-film (Wang et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2015; Du et al., 2015). Therefore, N-GQDs were capped on electrode surface with chitosan in this study. Using N-GQDs as luminescence label and catalyzer (Lin et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2014, 2015a, 2015b; Shen et al., 2012; Valizadeh et al., 2015), an ECL enhancement sensing system was designed ( Scheme 1). In the sensing system, N-GQDs were capped on electrode surface with chitosan. N-GQDs in the chitosan film can be used as catalysts for NA diazotization reaction. After the addition of NA, catalytic diazotization reaction occurred on the electrode (GCE) surface with the products of azide groups. Azide groups can lead to the ECL enhancement of N-GQDs due to the radical inducement. The main features of this sensing system were: (i) loading N-GQDs on the electrode surface with a facile process. (ii) As nanocatalyst, N-GQDs in the chitosan film can initiate NA diazotization reaction. (iii) The increase of N-GQDs ECL in the diazotization reaction can be used to detect NA. (iv) The sensing method was simple and straightforward, avoiding complex chemical reactions. To the best of our knowledge, it was the first reports that N-GQDs were used as both ECL label and catalyst in the nitroaniline detection. The proposed ECL sensor showed good stability, acceptable selectivity and reproducibility.

All chemicals and reagents were analytical grade and used directly without further purification. P-nitroaniline (NA) was purchased from Aldrich Chemical Co. Critic acid, ammonia (30% wt), carboxymethyl chitosan (90%), hydrochloric acid (37% wt), NaNO2 and other materials were obtained from Beijing Dinggguo Chemicals Co. (Beijing, China). All reagents were prepared using ultrapure water with a resistivity of greater than 18MΩcm−1. The water samples were collected from the underground water in Jilin University. All experiments were carried out at room temperature.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra measurements were performed on a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired on a UV-1200 UV–vis Spectrophotometer with a 1cm quart cell. Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was conducted on a Thermo Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrometer using KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted with a Thermo ESCALAB VG Scientific 250 spectrometer equipped with monochromatized Al Kα excitation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) experiments were performed on a Philips Tecnai F20 TEM operating at 200KV acceleration voltage. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image reported here was taken with an JSM-6700F instrument. The ECL detection system consisted of a BPCL ultraweak luminescence analyzer (Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing, China) and a CHI 660B electrochemical work-station (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., China). The voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was set at 900V during the whole process. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) analysis was performed on a CHI 660B electrochemical workstation in 2.5mmolL−1 K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6 with 0.1molL−1 KCl as the supporting electrolyte. A conventional three-electrode system was used. A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used as the wording electrode. A platinum wire was employed as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) electrode as the reference electrode.

N-GQDs were synthesized by carbonization of CA with ammonia according to the previous reports ( Chen et al., 2015). 2g of citric acid and 0.3ml of ammonia were transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 210°C for 1h. The resultant dark brown mixture (2g) was dissolved to 10ml with ultrapure water. Subsequently, pH of N-GQDs dispersion was tuned to 7.0 with adding NaOH solution dropwise. The supernatant was collected by removing the large dots through centrifugation at 12,000rpm for 10min. Then the obtained liquid was diluted to 20ml with water acquiring N-GQDs stock solution with concentration of 0.1g/ml. Finally, the as-prepared N-GQDs were stored at 4°C for further characterization and use.

Prior to modification, the basal GCE was carefully polished with the 1.0 and 0.05µm alumina slurry followed by rinsing thoroughly with double distilled water, and then ultrasonically cleaned in ethanol and double distilled water to obtain a mirror-like surface. Finally, it was dried under nitrogen atmosphere ready for use. 8μL of N-GQDs solution (0.01g/ml) was dropped onto the well-polished and cleaned bare GCE and evaporated in air (labeled as N-GQDs/GCE). Then 8μL of 0.25% (w/v) chitosan solution was dropped onto N-GQDs/GCE and dried in air (labeled as chitosan/N-GQDs/GCE). After every modifying step, the prepared electrode was washed with double distilled water. The sensor was stored in 0.1molL−1 pH 7.0 PBS in 4°C refrigerator when not used.

All electrochemical measurements were carried out on the aforementioned workstation with the three-electrode configuration at room temperature. A potential from 0.0 to −2.5V was applied to the working electrode and the ECL signal was recorded simultaneously. For the measurements of NA, the obtained chitosan/N-GQDs/GCE sensor was immersed in the electrolyte solution of 2mmolL−1 HCl and 10μmolL−1 NaNO2 containing different concentrations of NA to study the electrochemical response by ECL. ECL signals related to the NA concentration can be measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of the prepared N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1A showed the TEM image of the as-prepared N-GQDs in this work. It can be found that the resulting N-GQDs were relatively uniform. The PL spectra (Fig. 1B) of the resultant N-GQDs was collected. The N-GQDs exhibited a maximum emission at 440nm with an excitation wavelength of 353nm with quantum yield of 15.5%. And the inset photograph of Fig. 1B also displayed the strong blue PL emission of the resultant N-GQDs under the UV lamp (365nm). The surface chemistry of the N-GQDs was also identified by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S1A). The FT-IR spectrum showed the absorption of stretching vibration of C-OH at 1178, 1231cm−1, C=O at 1706cm−1. And the absorption peaks at 3439, 3062cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H. Peak at 1376cm−1 was corresponded to the C-NH in-plane bending vibration (Tam et al., 2014; Ju et al., 2014). To further explored the optical properties of N-GQDs, UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra were studied. As shown in Fig. S1B, a typical strong absorption peak at ca. 334nm was observed in UV–vis spectra, which was assigned to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain (Tam et al., 2014). Because the N-GQDs displayed excitation-dependent PL emission, the emission peak showed red-shift from 440 to 540nm by increasing the excitation wavelength from 330 to 480nm (Fig. S1B), which was relative to the size distribution of the N-GQDs (Lin et al., 2015). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed to determine the composition of the as-produced N-GQDs in Fig. S2A. Three peaks at 284.58, 400.75 and 531.34eV can be attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively (Wang et al., 2015a,2015b). The C 1s XPS can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.39, 285.11, 286.53, and 288.14eV (Fig. S2B), attributing to C-C, C-N, C-O, and C=O bonds, respectively (Li et al., 2012). The N 1s core level XPS spectra of N-GQDs present the C-N-C bond at 399.42eV, and N-(C)3 at 400.22eV (Fig. S2C) (Ju et al.,2014). In the high resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. S2D), two fitted peaks at 531.34 and 532.40eV were assigned to C=O and C-OH/C-O-C groups, respectively (Ju et al., 2014). These results confirmed the successful introduction of N in N-GQDs and were also consistent with the corresponding FT-IR spectroscopy results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization and electrochemiluminescence behavior of chitosan/N-GQDs system
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was performed to characterize the surface morphology of the modified electrode. Fig. 2A showed that a dense, uniform N-GQDs-chitosan film was fabricated and well-distributed on the electrode surface. In order to demonstrate the modification procedures of the electrode, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of modified electrodes in 2.5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was shown in Fig. 2B. The impedance spectra consist of a semicircle portion and a linear portion. The semicircle diameter at higher frequencies corresponds to the electron-transfer resistance (Ret). And the linear part at lower frequencies represents the diffusion process. As shown in Fig. 2B, it can be observed that the EIS of the bare GCE (curve a) displayed an almost straight line, which was characteristic of a mass diffusion limiting process. Compared with bare GCE, the diameter of the semicircle increased to about 390Ω when the N-GQDs were immobilized on the surface of GCE (curve b). With the further coating of chitosan onto N-GQDs/GCE, the electron-transfer resistance was increased to about 736Ω (curve c). The significant semicircle increase suggested the hindrance effect of the chitosan thin film to the electrical conductivity. The corresponding cyclic voltammograms (CVs) showed the decrease in peak current and the increase in peak-to-peak along with the ECL sensor fabrication process (Fig. 2C), which was in good agreement with the EIS studies.

The ECL signals of chitosan/N-GQDs/GCE sensor were measured with the different electrolyte solutions. In Fig. 2D, curve a and c showed the ECL signals of chitosan/N-GQDs/GCE electrode in the buffer and HCl solution, respectively. Because the N-GQD-based system can produce ECL with dissolved O2 as an efficient coreactant in acid medium, a significant ECL signal was observed within 10mmolL−1 HCl. There was a larger ECL signal of chitosan/N-GQDs/GCE sensor observed in the mixture of HCl and NaNO2 (curve d) compared to in HCl solution. It was due to the more active nitrosyl product of NaNO2. The possible ECL route was proposed as follows:
(1)

In order to confirm the ECL route of the proposed sensor, we added NA to the buffer solution (curve b) and mixed electrolyte solution of HCl/NaNO2 (curve d) and recorded ECL signals, respectively. Curve b showed that the addition of NA to buffer solution had no obvious effect on the ECL signal of sensing system. With the addition of NA to the mixed electrolyte solution of HCl and NaNO2, the ECL signal of the sensor enhanced remarkably (curve e). It indicated that the proposed ECL route was based on the diazotization reaction occurred among NA, HCl and NaNO2. According to recent catalysis researches about GQD and N-GQDs (Lin et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2014, 2015a, 2015b; Shen et al., 2012; Valizadeh et al., 2015), there had the diazotization reaction of NA on the modified electrode surface with N-GQDs catalyst. As the active mediates, the diazonium product can require faster electron transfer with N-GQDs to generate strong ECL signal. The reactions were shown as the following:
(7)

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimization of ECL detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve a high-performance ECL signal, the effects of HCl concentration, NaNO2 concentration, and scan rate on the ECL intensity of the chitosan/N-GQDs sensing system were investigated. As shown in Fig. S3, the ECL intensity increased with HCl concentration increasing from 1.0 to 2.0mmolL−1. Then the ECL signal decreased when HCl concentration was higher than 2.0mmolL−1. In the N-GQDs ECL sensing system, 2.0mmolL−1 HCl was chosen as optimal reaction condition. The experimental results expressed that the acidic condition was beneficial to convert NA into the diazo-compound and enhance the ECL intensity of N-GQDs. But the high concentration of HCl can destroy the stability of the chitosan film on the GCE. NaNO2 played the key role in the NA diazotization reaction. As shown in Fig. S4, the ECL signal increased following the increase of NaNO2 concentration from 0 to 10µmolL−1. With the continued increase of NaNO2 concentration from 10 to 50µmolL−1, the ECL signal decreased quickly. It was indicated that appropriate excess NaNO2 can restrain the self coupling of NA in the diazo reaction. When too much NaNO2 were added, the diazotization product of NA can be oxidized by NaNO2 in the acidic condition. So, NaNO2 of 10µmolL−1 was used in the ECL system. ECL efficiency significantly depended on the rate of generation/annihilation of excited-state N-GQDs. In Fig. S5, the ECL intensity decreased with the increasing scan rate from 0.1 to 0.7V/S. It indicated that higher scan rates were unfavorable to the electrochemical reaction of N-GQDs. In the following experiments, a scan rate of 0.1V/S was chosen to obtain the maximum ECL intensity.

In order to investigate the selectivity of this ECL sensor, we added different organic compounds in the chitosan/N-GQDs sensing system. As shown in Fig. 3, the ECL of chitosan/N-GQDs sensing system can keep stable with those organic compound, including 4-nitrochlorobenzene (2.56%), methylbenzene (2.05%), benzene (2.92%), hydroquinone (1.96%) and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (2.47%). Because the controlled reaction conditions of NA diazotization and specific ECL mechanism as above mentioned in Eqs. (7)–(10), other organic compounds can hardly proceed diazotization reaction with HCl and NaNO2. Therefore, other organic compounds had no obvious impact on the ECL signal of our sensing system. As a result, chitosan/N-GQDs sensing system had high selectivity to detect NA.

Under the optimal conditions, we investigated the relationship between the concentration and the response of the sensor by testing a series of standard solutions containing different NA concentrations varied from 0.01 to 1μmolL−1. As shown in Fig. 4A and B, the ECL intensity depended linearly on the logarithm of the NA concentration in the range from 0.01 to 1μmolL−1 with a lower detection limit (LOD) of 0.005μmolL−1. The linear regression equation was I=409.95lgc+1645.1, with a linear relation coefficient of 0.997. This enhancement of ECL can be explained by the Eqs. (7)–(10). With the increasing concentration of NA which was added in the proposed sensor, more diazonium products can be produced and further promoted the occurrence of reactions in Eqs. (9) and (10). And then resulting in the appearance of more N-GQDs* which gave an ECL signal-on continuously. To further affirm the superior performance of the established ECL sensor, some other reported methods for the determination of NA were compared with the proposed method. Table S1 clearly showed that the sensing system for NA detection described here was better than most of the reported methods.

To further illustrate the validity of the ECL sensor for NA, a systematic study of various interference components were added into the system when the determination of NA was carried out under the optimum conditions. Table S2 showed the interference effect and a relative error of ±5.0% was considered to be tolerable. The tolerable concentration ratios of coexisting substances was over 2000 fold for Ca2+, Mg2+, 1000 fold for Cl−, Na+, K+ and 500 fold for SO4 2−, NO3 −, methanol, ethanol, ether, acetonitrile, trichloromethane. With these high concentrations of commonly existing substances, there was little interference on the ECL of chitosan/N-GQDs sensing system. In order to assess the feasibility of the proposed ECL sensor, the amount of NA in water sample was detected. The results ( Table 1) obtained by standard addition method were shown in Table 1. The RSD was lower than 5% and the recoveries of NA in the real samples were between 95.9% and 104%.

In summary, we have developed a facile novel ECL signal-on method based on N-GQDs and chitosan film for NA sensing. The ECL signals of N-GQDs were enhanced by NA with HCl and NaNO2, successively. In addition, N-GQDs have shown as highly active reagent and catalyst for rapid diazotization reaction of anilines. A good linearity for NA detection in the range of 0.01–1μmolL−1 with the detection limit of 0.005μmolL−1 was found. Moreover, the novel sensing system has been employed to detect NA in real water sample with the satisfactory results. The ECL sensor for NA detection was high sensitive, selective and convenient in the real analysis application.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.06.010.
